paper: Knehr et al. - 2021 - Wafer-level uniformity of atomic-layer-deposited niobium nitride thin films for quantum devices

segments.text: 6,25,26 Due to the self-limiting growth process, ALD enables a precise thickness control on the monolayer-scale and excellent surface conformity.

27 The deposition process consists of alternating steps of the precursor ( tert -butylimido)-tris (diethylamido)-niobium (TBTDEN) and hydrogen radicals provided by an inductively coupled plasma (ICP) source, with purge steps in between.
The precursor provides both niobium and nitrogen atoms, while the hydrogen plasma reacts with the organic compounds (functional groups) of the precursor remaining on the substrate surface.
The by-products and residuals are purged in the subsequent step.
The substrate holder is heated to 380 /C14 C. A deposition cycle consists of the following steps: TBTDEN (3 s), N2-purge (7 s), hydrogen plasma (60 s, 8.3 mTorr, 300 W), and N2-purge (10 s).
First, we assume a constant niobium content over the whole area because of the self-limiting ALD process and the saturation of the surface with TBTDEN.
This would result in an extended area of uniform plasma distribution and reaction with the deposited precursor.
The precisely controllable ALD process enables uniform NbN films also of smaller thickness than the investigated 6 nm.

Together with the advantages of precise thickness control, conformal deposition, and moderate deposition temperatures below 400 /C14 C, 38 ALD-NbN thin films are suited for a versatile usage in various further applications, e.g., for back-end-of-line integration with photonic-integrated circuits, for which processing temperatures below (400 -500 /C14 C) are necessary.
